entities.description: A public electronic text keyed from Breul's printed pp.58-63. It serializes the English and French parallel columns as consecutive language-scoped versions and retains apparatus, pagination, five-line milestones, italic typography, and the article closer.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00120

rights.basis: The U-M item and collection apply No Copyright - United States to public content. This is a rights statement, not a license; provider service terms remain separate.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and represented public-domain source material

entities.description: An anonymous Middle English didactic courtesy poem in rhyming couplets, with a late-medieval line-aligned Middle French translation in the O/W/P bilingual branch. It teaches children table manners, cleanliness, deference, speech, charity, and social conduct.
entities.label: The Boke of Curtesy
notes: Breul dates the work broadly to the mid-fifteenth century and probably the English Midlands; later work-specific confirmation was not established in this review.
The anonymous French translation is a work/version layer; Pynson's surviving copy is Breul's French textual base, not a demonstrated translator's exemplar.

rights.basis: The work and translation are attested in fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century manuscripts and prints and predate modern copyright by centuries. Physical ownership, modern markup, catalogs, and digital-image terms are separate.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval Middle English poem and Middle French translation as historical textual works; physical objects and modern representations excluded

bibliographic.citation: Karl Breul, 'The Boke of Curtesy,' Englische Studien 9 (1886): 51-63.
bibliographic.title: The Boke of Curtesy

entities.description: A thirteen-page article with witness descriptions, editorial rationale, and an A-based text. The edition supplies five positions from E, collates H/E/C/O/W/P, prints a P-based French parallel with W variants, and closes with Breul's Cambridge date of 12 June 1885.
entities.label: Karl Breul, 'The Boke of Curtesy' (1886)
notes: DIMEV's bibliography gives the whole article as pp.51-63; its witness-A edition entry gives pp.58-63, exactly the span represented by CME00120.
The completed volume is dated 1886; 12 June 1885 is Breul's article dateline, not its publication date.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1886 and is in the U.S. public domain because works published before 1931 are outside the current U.S. copyright term in 2026. Internet Archive metadata independently labels the carrier public domain.
rights.component: Breul's 1886 article, edition, apparatus, and typography; later scan excluded

entities.description: A paper miscellany copied about 1480 in a North-Midland context. Richard Heeg's Boke of Curtesy occupies current ff.84v-86v and ends with his signed explicit; a different hand's medical recipe follows on the same f.86v surface.
entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.3.1 (Breul A)

physicalDescription.extent: xii + 216 + xviii folios; Boke of Curtesy at current ff.84v-86v
physicalDescription.format: Bound quarto miscellany in nine fascicles
notes: Full red morocco binding dated 1964.
NLS attributes the principal hands to Richard Heeg and John Hawghton; this component is signed by Heeg.
The work begins on f.84v and ends on the shared f.86v surface before a later recipe in another hand.

rights.basis: The witness was copied about 1480 and the underlying anonymous text predates modern copyright by centuries. Physical-access and image-reuse terms are separate.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper manuscript whose Boke of Curtesy is discontiguous and fragmentary: Breul describes an opening on f.210r-v and continuation from verse 60 on f.207r-v, with physical loss from the torn f.210 leaf.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 541 (Breul H)

physicalDescription.extent: Breul: f.210r-v and, from verse 60, f.207r-v; DIMEV: f.210r-v, incomplete
notes: Exact current foliation and the discontiguous continuation require direct verification.

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper manuscript with the poem at ff.58v-60r. Breul uses E to supply editorial positions 22 and 55-58 where A lacks text and collates it throughout.
entities.label: British Library, Egerton MS 1995 (Breul E)

entities.description: An early-sixteenth-century paper folio manuscript with 113 rows of the poem at ff.22v-23v. Henry Bradshaw's collation was printed by Furnivall, and Breul states that he checked the manuscript again.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Ee.4.35, Part I (Breul C)

physicalDescription.extent: Boke of Curtesy at ff.22v-23v; 113 rows reported by Breul

entities.description: A sixteenth-century bilingual manuscript with an English-French Boke of Curtesy on pp.289-291. Breul's O readings came through E. Hausknecht's O/W collation rather than a claimed direct inspection by Breul.
entities.label: Balliol College, MS 354 (Breul O)

notes: The manuscript gives interlinear English and French versions.

bibliographic.citation: Here begynneth a lytell treatyse for to lerne Englysshe and Frensshe ([Westminster: Wynkyn de Worde, about 1497]).
bibliographic.pages: 12 leaves; Boke of Curtesy at ff.7r-10v
bibliographic.title: Here begynneth a lytell treatyse for to lerne Englysshe and Frensshe

entities.description: An early bilingual print, conventionally dated about 1497, with the Boke of Curtesy at ff.7r-10v. Breul directly transcribed W and used its English and French readings in his apparatus.
entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde, Here begynneth a lytell treatyse for to lerne Englysshe and Frensshe (Breul W)

bibliographic.citation: A Lytell Treatyse for to Lerne Englysshe and Frensshe ([London: Richard Pynson, about 1500]).
bibliographic.pages: 11 leaves; Boke of Curtesy at ff.6v-10r
bibliographic.title: A Lytell Treatyse for to Lerne Englysshe and Frensshe

entities.description: An early bilingual print dated about 1500, surviving without its title leaf. The Boke of Curtesy occupies ff.6v-10r. Breul collated it against W and uses P as the base of his printed French parallel.
entities.label: Richard Pynson, A Lytell Treatyse for to Lerne Englysshe and Frensshe (Breul P)

entities.description: A manuscript witness at ff.201v-203v cataloged by DIMEV v1.0 but absent from Breul's seven-source account.
entities.label: British Library, Additional MS 8151

bibliographic.citation: A good boke to lerne to speke French ([London: Robert Wyer?, about 1533]).
bibliographic.title: A good boke to lerne to speke French

entities.description: A fragmentary bilingual print conventionally assigned to Robert Wyer about 1533 and cataloged by DIMEV as a ninth witness; it was not among Breul's seven sources.
entities.label: A good boke to lerne to speke French (STC 24868)

entities.description: The working collation of Balliol O against Wynkyn de Worde W communicated to Breul. It is modeled as an intermediary scholarly record so O readings are not falsely described as Breul's direct manuscript transcription.
entities.label: E. Hausknecht's O/W collation for Breul
notes: No surviving public copy or present repository for the working collation was established from Breul's article or DIMEV.

next_steps: Consult recent editions and dialect studies.
Do not substitute witness A's c.1480 North-Midland copying context for the work's composition event.
openQuestions.question: What later linguistic and work-specific scholarship can confirm or revise Breul's mid-fifteenth-century, Midlands composition estimate?

next_steps: Collate complete O, W, and P bilingual versions and consult later phrasebook scholarship.
Keep Breul's P-based editorial French text distinct from the historical translation event.
openQuestions.question: Who translated the poem into Middle French, when and where, and from which English version or exemplar?

next_steps: Consult post-Breul collations including Add.8151 and STC 24868.
Do not infer direct witness-to-witness derivation from Breul's edition-source relations.
openQuestions.question: What stemma or redaction history connects all nine DIMEV witnesses?

next_steps: Inspect Harley MS 541 directly or obtain current folio images.
Reconcile historical and current foliation before normalizing the complete H locus.
openQuestions.question: Does current BL foliation confirm Breul's discontiguous H continuation at f.207r-v in addition to the DIMEV-indexed f.210r-v fragment?

next_steps: Search Breul, Hausknecht, and Arnold archival papers and correspondence.
Retain the intermediaries explicitly even while their present survival is unknown.
openQuestions.question: Do Hausknecht's O/W collation or Arnold's W French-variant notes survive, and if so where?

next_steps: Request a path-specific clarification from the repository maintainer.
Until clarified, preserve copyright status as unknown for the modern XML layer and do not broaden the software wording.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally apply to XML markup and data?

subject.description: Witness edition entry and Bibliography.xml Breul1886b

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00120.xml. The abstract work, witness objects, print article, facsimiles, U-M encoding, and later DIMEV catalog record are separate entities.

The U-M encoding starts at the edition heading partway down printed p.58 (PDF page 74) and continues through the French ending and article closer on p.63 (PDF page 79). It omits pp.51-57 and the source discussion at the top of p.58, as well as the rest of Englische Studien volume 9.

Breul prints 112 Middle English editorial positions. A lacks line 22 and lines 55-58, which he supplies from E, and has a later duplicate of line 37 that he declines to repeat; A therefore has 108 written lines, matching DIMEV's fifty-four-couplet description.

The English apparatus uses sigla A, H, E, C, O, W, and P. Breul inspected A, H, E, C, W, and P in stated ways; O evidence reached him through E. Hausknecht's O/W collation. Franck Arnold communicated the W variants used under the P-based French column.
The two XML DIV2 elements are parallel language versions, not numbered medieval work sections. The closing Cambridge date and Breul signature are modern article paratext.

Current NLS ff.84v-86v correspond to canvases and ZIP images 184-188. Canvas 183 closes Sir Amadas; 184 begins 'Lytyll chyldur'; 188 has the explicit followed by another hand's recipe on the same surface; 189 begins the following work.

The NLS Presentation Canvas identifiers for 184 and 188 returned HTTP 404. The recorded start and end IIIF Image API URLs returned unauthenticated HTTP 200 image/jpeg responses and are the actionable digital boundaries.
The NLS cache is complete relative to all 482 canvases in the provider manifest, not every physical leaf: the provider notes that numerous modern blank opening flyleaves were not all captured.

The repository root LICENSE is the software-worded Unlicense. It contains no XML- or data-specific scope statement, so applicability to CME00120 markup/data is recorded as unknown rather than broadened into a confirmed public-domain dedication.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00120, keyed from printed pp.58-63 of Karl Breul's article 'The Boke of Curtesy' in Englische Studien 9 (1886), pp.51-63. Breul's Middle English text is based on manuscript A, NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1, current ff.84v-86v, with five line positions supplied from E and variants from H, E, C, O, W, and P. His French parallel is transcribed from Pynson's P and collated with Wynkyn de Worde's W. The complete 526-page Internet Archive volume, complete OCR and metadata, the complete 482-canvas NLS digital object, and complete DIMEV v1.0 release are cached with fixity. DIMEV records nine witnesses, adding BL Add.8151 and a Wyer fragment to the seven sources known to Breul.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Mid-fifteenth century according to Breul; first securely attested in late-fifteenth-century witnesses; source article published 1886
catalogSummary.region: England, probably the Midlands according to Breul; witness A was copied about 1480 in a North-Midland context
catalogSummary.title: The Boke of Curtesy

catalogingSubject.label: The Boke of Curtesy
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English courtesy poem together with the early line-aligned Middle French translation transmitted in the bilingual O/W/P branch. It is not NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1 as a physical manuscript, Karl Breul's 1886 article as a whole, the Internet Archive scan, the University of Michigan electronic text, or the checked-in XML artifact. CME00120 represents Breul's printed pp.58-63: an A-based 112-position Middle English text, Pynson-based French parallel, critical apparatus, and editorial closer.

contentParts.label: Middle English edited text

locators.value: first DIV2, LANG='enm'; one LG and 112 L

notes: The heading and two version DIV2 elements are edition/encoding structures, not evidence for numbered medieval sections.
contentParts.summary: Breul's A-based 112-position text, with line 22 and lines 55-58 supplied from E, A-only lines 95-98 bracketed, and a trailer naming Hyheg.

contentParts.label: Middle French parallel translation

locators.value: French column on pp.58-63; Internet Archive PDF pages 74-79
second DIV2, LANG='fre'; six P elements and 107 nonempty bar-separated display segments

notes: The 107 segments include omission statements and are representation metrics, not a claim that the translation has 107 verse lines.

dateStatements.display: Late fifteenth century; witness A is cataloged about 1480 and the early printed bilingual witnesses date about 1497-c.1500
By the earliest bilingual manuscript and print witnesses of the late fifteenth century

extent.description: The 136 modeled content items comprise one English poem, six French paragraphs, 128 apparatus notes, and one editor closer. The XML has one poem DIV1 containing two language-scoped DIV2 version units, not two numbered work sections. The English version's single LG has 112 L elements, 105 notes, six PB elements, 22 five-line milestones, one HEAD, and one TRAILER. The French version has six P elements, 23 notes, six PB elements, and 19 milestones; its paragraphs contain 107 nonempty bar-separated display segments, including printed omission markers. Across the file are 128 NOTE, 12 PB, 41 MILESTONE, 643 italic HI, and one editor CLOSER with DATELINE and SIGNED. The 112 XML lines are Breul's editorial positions; manuscript A has 108 written lines.

formStatements.description: A didactic poem in rhyming couplets; DIMEV describes witness A as fifty-four couplets.
Breul's line-aligned French translation is serialized in six XML paragraph elements with vertical bars separating translated positions.

scope.description: Original courtesy poem and the principal version represented by the edition

languageStatements.label: Middle French
notes: The XML uses legacy LANG='fre'; frm is the normalized historical-language code.
TEXT/@LANG='enm fre' describes the represented medieval language layers. German occurs in Breul's apparatus and modern editorial explanation but is not a language of the abstract work.
The XML proofing date 9-9-16 is repository encoding history, not a work, witness, edition, or publication date.
The two DIV2 elements are parallel language versions. They are not numbered work sections, and the closing dateline/signature is modern article paratext.
Current NLS foliation and direct visual boundaries are used for witness A. Breul's older foliation 84v-86v agrees in this case, but exact canvas boundaries are independently verified.

next_steps: Consult current work-specific linguistic and courtesy-book scholarship.
Keep work composition distinct from the c.1480 copying date and North-Midland context of witness A.
openQuestions.question: What evidence beyond Breul's 1886 dialectal judgment can refine the poem's date and place of composition?

next_steps: Collate the complete bilingual O, W, and P texts and consult later phrasebook scholarship.
Do not infer Pynson's surviving print as the translator's physical exemplar merely because Breul uses it for his French column.
openQuestions.question: Who produced the Middle French translation, when and where was it made, and which English version or exemplar did it translate?

next_steps: Consult post-Breul editions and collations of all nine DIMEV witnesses.
Preserve Breul's source use and later DIMEV witness discovery as separate evidence layers.
openQuestions.question: What stemmatic or redactional relationships connect A, H, E, C, O, W, P, the Add.8151 witness, and the Wyer fragment?

next_steps: Inspect the current British Library manuscript and foliation directly.
Treat DIMEV's locus as an indexed opening fragment until the continuation is verified against current foliation.
openQuestions.question: How should Breul's discontiguous Harley text at current or historical ff.210r-v and 207r-v be reconciled with DIMEV's shorter f.210r-v locus?

next_steps: Seek a path-specific data or XML rights statement from the repository maintainer.
Until clarified, do not broaden the root software wording into a confirmed public-domain grant for markup or data.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally apply to the XML markup and data in CME00120?

notes: Breul's dialectal inference, kept separate from the place of copying any particular witness.

scope.description: Modern edition layers represented by CME00120

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English courtesy poem instructing children in cleanliness, restraint, table manners, deference, speech, charity, and conduct before, during, and after a meal. A late-medieval bilingual branch aligns the poem with a Middle French translation.

CME00120 starts at the 'The Boke of Curtesy' edition heading partway down printed p.58 and continues through the French ending and Cambridge 12 June 1885 closer on p.63. It omits pp.51-57 and the source discussion at the top of p.58, as well as the rest of Englische Studien volume 9.

Breul prints 112 editorial Middle English line positions. His notes show that A lacks position 22 and positions 55-58, supplied from E, while A contains one later duplicate of line 37 that he does not print again. A therefore has 108 written lines, agreeing with DIMEV's fifty-four-couplet description; these are different measurements, not competing extents.

notes: Witness A has no opening title; Breul explains that the title is supported by the majority of the other versions.
titles.supplied_by: Breul's edition and titles in most witnesses

notes: A title-like phrase reported from witness E rather than an authorial title.

work.author: Anonymous

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English courtesy poem instructing children in cleanliness, restraint, table manners, deference, speech, charity, and conduct before, during, and after a meal. A late-medieval bilingual branch aligns the poem with a Middle French translation.
